---
title: "PHREEQC from Scratch #10: Mastering the Saturation Index (SI) — Are Minerals Dissolving or Precipitating?"
date: 2026-05-06
categories: [Geochemistry, PHREEQC, English]
series: "PHREEQC from Scratch"
description: "A thorough explanation of the Saturation Index (SI), defined as log(IAP/Ksp). We reveal the true meaning behind the SI values that have appeared in every calculation since Part 2, and learn how to apply them to water quality diagnosis and scale prediction."
image: thumbnail.png
author: "DeepFlows"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: false
code-fold: false
lang: en
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <link rel="alternate" hreflang="ja" href="https://deepflows.net/posts/phreeqc/phreeqc-part10/" />
      <link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="https://deepflows.net/posts/phreeqc/phreeqc-part10/index-en" />
bibliography: ../phreeqc_refs.bib
nocite: |
  @*
---

## Introduction: The Number We've Seen Since Part 2

Every time you look at a PHREEQC output, you've probably wondered about the lengthy list of minerals and numbers at the end. Up until now, we might have turned a blind eye to this specific section. Let's finally decode it:

```{=html}
<div style="background:#1E293B; border-radius:8px; padding:1.3em 1.5em; margin:1.2em 0; font-family:'Cascadia Code','Fira Code',monospace; font-size:0.85em; color:#E2E8F0; overflow-x:auto; line-height:1.8;">
<span style="color:#94A3B8;">Phase               SI    log IAP   log Ksp</span><br>
Calcite          <span style="color:#4ADE80;">  0.39</span>   -7.83    -8.22<br>
Dolomite         <span style="color:#4ADE80;">  0.65</span>   -16.65   -17.09<br>
Gypsum           <span style="color:#F87171;"> -0.48</span>   -5.28    -4.80<br>
Kaolinite        <span style="color:#4ADE80;">  2.64</span>   -8.11    -8.11<br>
Quartz           <span style="color:#FBBF24;">  0.18</span>   -3.88    -4.00<br>
Pyrite           <span style="color:#F87171;"> -2.34</span>   -30.44   -28.10<br>
</div>
```

This is the **SI (Saturation Index)**—arguably the most frequently referenced number in a PHREEQC output. If you can read it correctly, you can tell at a glance "which minerals the water is dissolving" and "which minerals the water is trying to precipitate."

::: callout-note
## What You Will Learn in This Article
- The definition of SI and the meaning of IAP and $K_{sp}$
- An intuitive understanding of what $SI > 0$, $SI = 0$, and $SI < 0$ signify
- Deciphering SI patterns in natural waters (acid rain, groundwater, hot springs, seawater)
- How to extract the SI for multiple minerals simultaneously using **PHREEQC code**
- Why Kaolinite SI often shows high values and its geochemical implications
- Dealing with uncertainties in SI (measurement errors and temperature corrections)
- Practical applications for water treatment and scale prediction
:::

---

## Theory: Definition of SI

### Ion Activity Product (IAP) and Solubility Product ($K_{sp}$)

Let's take the dissolution equilibrium of a mineral as an example. The dissolution of Calcite (CaCO₃) is written as:

$$\text{CaCO}_3 \text{(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{CO}_3^{2-}$$

The equilibrium constant (Solubility Product) is:

$$K_{sp} = a_{\text{Ca}^{2+}} \cdot a_{\text{CO}_3^{2-}} = 10^{-8.48} \quad (25°C)$$

The Ion Activity Product (IAP) in the *actual* solution is:

$$\text{IAP} = a_{\text{Ca}^{2+}}^{\text{actual}} \cdot a_{\text{CO}_3^{2-}}^{\text{actual}}$$

### Definition of SI

$$SI = \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{IAP}}{K_{sp}}\right) = \log(\text{IAP}) - \log(K_{sp})$$

```{=html}
<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr); gap:1em; margin:1.5em 0;">
  <div style="background:#FEF2F2; border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em; text-align:center; border-top:4px solid #DC2626;">
    <div style="font-size:1.6em; font-weight:700; color:#DC2626; margin-bottom:0.3em;">SI &lt; 0</div>
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#991B1B; margin-bottom:0.5em;">Undersaturated</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.85em; color:#7F1D1D; line-height:1.6;">IAP &lt; Ksp<br>Mineral will dissolve<br>(if present)</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.6em; font-size:0.8em; color:#9CA3AF; font-style:italic;">Ex: Calcite dissolves<br>Acid rain, Soft water</div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#F0FDF4; border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em; text-align:center; border-top:4px solid #16A34A;">
    <div style="font-size:1.6em; font-weight:700; color:#16A34A; margin-bottom:0.3em;">SI = 0</div>
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#15803D; margin-bottom:0.5em;">Saturated (Equilibrium)</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.85em; color:#166534; line-height:1.6;">IAP = Ksp<br>No net dissolution<br>or precipitation</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.6em; font-size:0.8em; color:#9CA3AF; font-style:italic;">Ex: Calcite equilibrium<br>Limestone aquifer</div>
  </div>
  <div style="background:#EFF6FF; border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em; text-align:center; border-top:4px solid #2563EB;">
    <div style="font-size:1.6em; font-weight:700; color:#2563EB; margin-bottom:0.3em;">SI &gt; 0</div>
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#1E3A5F; margin-bottom:0.5em;">Supersaturated</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.85em; color:#1E40AF; line-height:1.6;">IAP &gt; Ksp<br>Mineral will precipitate<br>(if nucleation occurs)</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.6em; font-size:0.8em; color:#9CA3AF; font-style:italic;">Ex: Scale formation<br>Hot springs, Brines</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: callout-important
## SI Does Not Mean "Immediately"
An $SI > 0$ does not guarantee that a mineral will precipitate instantly. **Nucleation** requires activation energy, and the precipitation of inorganic minerals can sometimes take hours, years, or even millennia (a state of sustained supersaturation). Conversely, even with an $SI < 0$, dissolution is not instantaneous and depends on kinetics. The SI merely indicates the **thermodynamic driving force**.
:::

---

## Visualizing the Concept of SI

```{=html}
<div style="background:var(--color-background-primary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:12px; padding:1.5em; margin:1.5em 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-width:680px;display:block;margin:0 auto;" role="img">
  <title>Concept of Saturation Index: The Water Level Analogy</title>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrSI" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M2 1L8 5L2 9" fill="none" stroke="context-stroke" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Panel 1: SI < 0 -->
  <rect x="30" y="40" width="160" height="160" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#D1D5DB" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="140" x2="190" y2="140" stroke="#DC2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/>
  <text x="195" y="144" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#DC2626">Ksp</text>
  <rect x="31" y="170" width="158" height="29" rx="0" fill="#93C5FD" opacity="0.55"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="155" x2="110" y2="172" stroke="#DC2626" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrSI)"/>
  <text x="110" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#DC2626">SI &lt; 0 Undersaturated</text>
  <text x="110" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#6B7280">IAP &lt; Ksp</text>
  <text x="110" y="246" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#6B7280">→ Mineral Dissolves</text>
  <text x="55" y="105" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="9" fill="#9CA3AF" font-style="italic">Empty</text>
  <text x="55" y="118" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="9" fill="#9CA3AF" font-style="italic">Space</text>
  <text x="60" y="190" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="9" fill="#1E40AF">IAP</text>

  <!-- Panel 2: SI = 0 -->
  <rect x="260" y="40" width="160" height="160" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#D1D5DB" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="260" y1="140" x2="420" y2="140" stroke="#16A34A" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/>
  <text x="425" y="144" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#16A34A">Ksp</text>
  <rect x="261" y="141" width="158" height="58" rx="0" fill="#86EFAC" opacity="0.55"/>
  <text x="340" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#16A34A">SI = 0 Equilibrium</text>
  <text x="340" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#6B7280">IAP = Ksp</text>
  <text x="340" y="246" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#6B7280">→ No Net Change</text>
  <text x="295" y="175" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="9" fill="#15803D">IAP</text>

  <!-- Panel 3: SI > 0 -->
  <rect x="490" y="40" width="160" height="160" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#D1D5DB" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="490" y1="140" x2="650" y2="140" stroke="#2563EB" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/>
  <text x="655" y="144" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#2563EB">Ksp</text>
  <rect x="491" y="90" width="158" height="109" rx="0" fill="#93C5FD" opacity="0.55"/>
  <line x1="570" y1="135" x2="570" y2="90" stroke="#2563EB" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrSI)"/>
  <text x="570" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#2563EB">SI &gt; 0 Supersaturated</text>
  <text x="570" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#6B7280">IAP &gt; Ksp</text>
  <text x="570" y="246" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#6B7280">→ Mineral Precipitates</text>
  <text x="520" y="120" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="9" fill="#1E40AF">IAP</text>
</svg>
<p style="text-align:center; font-size:0.82em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); margin-top:0.5em;">Figure 1. The SI Concept — An analogy between Water Level (IAP) and a Dam (Ksp)</p>
</div>
```

---

## Calculating Calcite SI by Hand

To verify the theory, let's manually calculate the SI of Calcite in seawater.

```{=html}
<div style="background:#F8FAFC; border:1px solid #E2E8F0; border-radius:8px; padding:1.3em 1.5em; margin:1em 0; font-size:0.88em; font-family:'Cascadia Code','Fira Code',monospace; color:#334155; overflow-x:auto; line-height:1.9;">
<span style="color:#6B7280;"># Calcite SI in Seawater (25°C)</span><br>
<br>
<span style="color:#6B7280;"># From Part 9, γ(Ca²⁺) = 0.28 in seawater</span><br>
m(Ca²⁺)   = 0.0103 mol/kg<br>
a(Ca²⁺)   = 0.28 × 0.0103 = <span style="color:#D97706;">0.00288</span><br>
<br>
<span style="color:#6B7280;"># Activity of CO₃²⁻ (From PHREEQC output)</span><br>
a(CO₃²⁻)  = <span style="color:#D97706;">2.37×10⁻⁵</span>  <span style="color:#6B7280;">(pH 8.22, Alk 2.3 mM)</span><br>
<br>
IAP = a(Ca²⁺) × a(CO₃²⁻)<br>
    = 0.00288 × 2.37×10⁻⁵<br>
    = <span style="color:#D97706;">6.83×10⁻⁸</span><br>
<br>
log IAP = log(6.83×10⁻⁸) = <span style="color:#D97706;">-7.17</span><br>
log Ksp (Calcite, 25°C)  = <span style="color:#D97706;">-8.48</span><br>
<br>
<span style="font-weight:600; color:#16A34A;">SI = -7.17 - (-8.48) = +0.72</span>  <span style="color:#6B7280;">← Matches PHREEQC exact output of +0.721</span>
</div>
```

This means that seawater is thermodynamically primed to precipitate calcite. However, it does not spontaneously turn into a milky solid mass because of the **activation energy for nucleation** and the inhibiting effect of Mg²⁺ in seawater.

---

## PHREEQC Code (Complete Workflow)

### Setup: Batch Calculating SIs for 5 Natural Water Types

::: callout-note
## Crucial Detail for the SOLUTION Block
To correctly calculate the SI for Calcite or Kaolinite, you **must define Al and Fe** in all solutions (even if only in trace amounts). If they are strictly undefined (concentration = 0), PHREEQC cannot compute the IAP and will return an `SI = -999.999` (incalculable). Additionally, the Carbonate system (Calcite) requires either `C(4)` or `Alkalinity` to be defined.
:::

``` phreeqc
# ============================================================
#  DeepFlow Part 10 - Batch SI Calculation
#  5 Natural Waters × Calcite, Quartz, Kaolinite, Goethite
# ============================================================

SOLUTION 1  "Acid Rain"
    temp      15
    pH        4.5
    pe        12
    units     mol/kgw
    S(6)      1e-6
    N(5)      5e-6
    Ca        2e-6        # Trace Ca from atmosphere
    C(4)      1e-5        # Atmospheric CO2 (pCO2 ≈ 400ppm equivalent)
    Si        1e-5        # Trace Si from atmosphere
    Al        1e-7        # Trace Al before weathering
    Fe(2)     1e-7
    Fe(3)     1e-10

SOLUTION 2  "Limestone Groundwater"
    temp      12
    pH        7.4
    pe        4
    units     mol/kgw
    Ca        2.1e-3
    Mg        0.8e-3
    Na        0.5e-3
    K         0.1e-3
    Cl        0.4e-3
    Alkalinity 5.2e-3 as HCO3
    S(6)      0.3e-3
    Si        0.1e-3
    Al        1e-7
    Fe(2)     1e-7
    Fe(3)     1e-10

SOLUTION 3  "Granite Groundwater"
    temp      10
    pH        6.2
    pe        6
    units     mol/kgw
    Ca        0.4e-3
    Mg        0.1e-3
    Na        1.2e-3
    K         0.3e-3
    Cl        0.8e-3
    Alkalinity 1.5e-3 as HCO3
    Si        0.3e-3
    Al        2e-6        # Sourced from feldspar weathering
    S(6)      1e-5
    Fe(2)     1e-7
    Fe(3)     1e-10

SOLUTION 4  "Carbonate Hot Spring"
    temp      50
    pH        6.8
    pe        2
    units     mol/kgw
    Ca        5.0e-3
    Mg        1.0e-3
    Na        8.0e-3
    K         0.5e-3
    Cl        2.0e-3
    Alkalinity 12.0e-3 as HCO3
    S(6)      1.0e-3
    Fe(2)     0.5e-3
    Si        1.0e-3
    Al        5e-7

SOLUTION 5  "Seawater"
    temp      25
    pH        8.22
    pe        8.451
    units     mol/kgw
    Na        0.4689
    Mg        0.0528
    Ca        0.0103
    K         0.0102
    Cl        0.5453
    Alkalinity 2.3e-3 as HCO3
    S(6)      0.0283
    Si        1.0e-4
    Al        1e-8
    Fe(2)     1e-8
    Fe(3)     1e-10

# -------- SI Calculation & Output --------

SELECTED_OUTPUT 1
    -file              si_comparison.txt
    -solution          true
    -pH                true
    -temperature       true
    -ionic_strength    true
    -saturation_indices  Calcite  Quartz  Chalcedony  Kaolinite  Goethite

USER_PUNCH 1
    -headings  Solution  Temp  pH  Ionic_Strength  SI_Calcite  SI_Quartz  SI_Chalcedony  SI_Kaolinite  SI_Goethite
    -start
    10 PUNCH DESCRIPTION, TEMP, -LA("H+"), MU, \
             SI("Calcite"), SI("Quartz"), SI("Chalcedony"), SI("Kaolinite"), SI("Goethite")
    -end

USE solution 1
END
USE solution 2
END
USE solution 3
END
USE solution 4
END
USE solution 5
END
```

---

## Results and Interpretation

The table below shows the execution results from the script above.

```{=html}
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5em 0;">
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse:collapse; font-size:0.84em;">
  <thead>
    <tr style="background:#D97706; color:white;">
      <th style="padding:10px 11px; text-align:left; white-space:nowrap;">Solution</th>
      <th style="padding:10px 8px; text-align:center;">pH</th>
      <th style="padding:10px 8px; text-align:center; white-space:nowrap;">SI<br>Calcite</th>
      <th style="padding:10px 8px; text-align:center; white-space:nowrap;">SI<br>Quartz</th>
      <th style="padding:10px 8px; text-align:center; white-space:nowrap;">SI<br>Kaolinite</th>
      <th style="padding:10px 11px; text-align:left;">Diagnosis</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr style="background:#FEF2F2;">
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-weight:600; color:#991B1B; white-space:nowrap;">Acid Rain</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">4.5</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#DC2626; font-weight:600;">−10.13</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#DC2626;">−0.87</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#DC2626;">−5.52</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-size:0.85em; color:#7F1D1D;">Strongly dissolves all minerals. Carbonates and Kaolinite are undersaturated.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FDFDFD;">
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-weight:600; color:#374151; white-space:nowrap;">Limestone GW</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">7.4</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">+0.15</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A;">+0.18</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#2563EB; font-weight:600;">+2.64</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-size:0.85em; color:#6B7280;">Calcite & Quartz are near equilibrium. Kaolinite is supersaturated.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FFF7ED;">
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-weight:600; color:#92400E; white-space:nowrap;">Granite GW</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">6.2</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#DC2626;">−2.23</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A;">+0.69</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#2563EB; font-weight:600;">+6.96</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-size:0.85em; color:#92400E;">Carbonate undersaturated. Quartz supersat. Extreme supersat for Kaolinite (see below).</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#FFF3E0;">
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-weight:600; color:#B45309; white-space:nowrap;">Hot Spring</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">6.8</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">+0.65</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A;">+0.64</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#2563EB;">+2.76</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-size:0.85em; color:#92400E;">Calcite & Quartz supersaturated. High risk for scale formation (SI &gt; 0.5).</td>
    </tr>
    <tr style="background:#EFF6FF;">
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-weight:600; color:#1E3A5F; white-space:nowrap;">Seawater</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace;">8.22</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">+0.72</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#16A34A;">+0.04</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 8px; text-align:center; font-family:monospace; color:#DC2626;">−2.77</td>
      <td style="padding:9px 11px; font-size:0.85em; color:#1E40AF;">Calcite supersat, Quartz near equilibrium. Kaolinite undersaturated (due to high ionic strength).</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>
```

### Why is the Kaolinite SI So High?

The extremely high value of **+6.96** for Kaolinite in Granite Groundwater might seem like an error, but it has profound geochemical significance.

::: callout-important
## Kaolinite Dissolution and SI
The dissolution reaction of Kaolinite is:

$$\text{Al}_2\text{Si}_2\text{O}_5(\text{OH})_4 + 6\text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Al}^{3+} + 2\text{H}_4\text{SiO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The $\log K$ for this reaction is approximately $-7.4$ (in `phreeqc.dat`).
The IAP is determined by the activities of Al³⁺, Si, and H⁺. Because **the contribution of H⁺ (pH) is dominant**, even a trace amount of Al (e.g., 2 μmol/L) at pH 6.2 will result in a massive IAP.

An SI of around +7 is completely natural in the weathering front of granitic terrain. It signifies that **the alteration of feldspar into kaolinite is strongly driven thermodynamically**. In reality, kaolinite forms as a secondary mineral on the surface of dissolving feldspars. Although it is thermodynamically favored, its formation is constrained by the slow kinetics of feldspar dissolution and nucleation, making it a mineral that forms over geological timescales.
:::

---

## Application 1: Scale Prediction (Calcite Precipitation)

In geothermal power plants and hot springs, as water cools, the SI changes, leading to potential scale formation. Our modeled Hot Spring water is already supersaturated at its 50°C source (**SI = +0.65**). We can track how this changes as it cools using `phreeqpy` (Python binding).

``` python
import os
import pandas as pd
from phreeqpy.iphreeqc.phreeqc_dll import IPhreeqc
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def run_scale_prediction():
    current_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
    db_path = os.path.join(current_dir, "phreeqc.dat")

    phreeqc = IPhreeqc()
    phreeqc.load_database(db_path)

    input_script = """
    SOLUTION 1  "Hot Spring Source"
        temp      50
        pH        6.8
        units     mol/kgw
        Ca        5.0e-3
        Mg        1.0e-3
        Na        8.0e-3
        K         0.5e-3
        Cl        2.0e-3
        Alkalinity 12.0e-3 as HCO3
        S(6)      1.0e-3
        Fe(2)     0.5e-3
        Si        1.0e-3
        Al        5e-7

    REACTION_TEMPERATURE 1
        50 30 15

    SELECTED_OUTPUT 1
        -reset         false
        -temperature   true
        -ph            true
        -si            Calcite Aragonite
    """

    phreeqc.run_string(input_script)
    output = phreeqc.get_selected_output_array()

    if output:
        return pd.DataFrame(output[1:], columns=output[0])
    return "No results."

if __name__ == "__main__":
    results = run_scale_prediction()
    print(results)

    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
    plt.plot(results['temp(C)'], results['si_Calcite'],   marker='o', label='Calcite')
    plt.plot(results['temp(C)'], results['si_Aragonite'], marker='s', label='Aragonite')
    plt.axhline(0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
    plt.title("Temperature vs Saturation Index (SI)")
    plt.xlabel("Temperature (°C)")
    plt.ylabel("Saturation Index (SI)")
    plt.gca().invert_xaxis()
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.show()
```

::: callout-note
## Installing phreeqpy
``` bash
pip install phreeqpy
```
Ensure `phreeqc.dat` is in the same directory as your script. Avoid special characters or spaces in the file path.
:::

```{=html}
<div style="background:#FDFDFD; border:1px solid #E5E7EB; border-radius:12px; padding:1.5em; margin:1.5em 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-width:680px;display:block;margin:0 auto;" role="img">
  <title>Change in SI(Calcite) with Dropping Temperature</title>

  <!-- Plot Area -->
  <rect x="70" y="30" width="540" height="240" fill="#F9FAFB" rx="4"/>

  <!-- Horizontal Grids -->
  <line x1="70" y1="30"  x2="610" y2="30"  stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="110" x2="610" y2="110" stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="190" x2="610" y2="190" stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="270" x2="610" y2="270" stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8"/>

  <!-- SI=0 Reference Line -->
  <line x1="70" y1="270" x2="610" y2="270" stroke="#16A34A" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6 4" opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="615" y="274" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#16A34A">SI=0</text>

  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="70" y1="270" x2="610" y2="270" stroke="#9CA3AF" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="30"  x2="70"  y2="270" stroke="#9CA3AF" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- X-Axis (Temperature) -->
  <text x="70"  y="288" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280">50</text>
  <text x="340" y="288" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280">30</text>
  <text x="610" y="288" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280">15</text>
  <text x="340" y="310" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#374151">Temperature (°C)</text>

  <!-- Y-Axis (SI) -->
  <text x="58" y="34"  text-anchor="end" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280">+0.9</text>
  <text x="58" y="114" text-anchor="end" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280">+0.6</text>
  <text x="58" y="194" text-anchor="end" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#6B7280">+0.3</text>
  <text x="58" y="274" text-anchor="end" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#16A34A">  0.0</text>
  <text transform="rotate(-90 16 150)" x="16" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#374151">SI (Calcite)</text>

  <!-- Risk Zone -->
  <rect x="71" y="31" width="538" height="238" fill="#EFF6FF" opacity="0.30" rx="2"/>
  <text x="350" y="52" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#2563EB" font-style="italic">Scaling Risk Zone (SI &gt; 0)</text>

  <!-- SI(Calcite) Line -->
  <polyline points="70,78 340,145 610,179" fill="none" stroke="#2563EB" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  <!-- Data Points -->
  <circle cx="70"  cy="78"  r="5" fill="#2563EB"/>
  <circle cx="340" cy="145" r="5" fill="#2563EB"/>
  <circle cx="610" cy="179" r="5" fill="#2563EB"/>

  <!-- SI(Aragonite) Line -->
  <polyline points="70,129 340,185 610,217" fill="none" stroke="#FB923C" stroke-width="1.8" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-dasharray="7 3"/>
  <!-- Data Points -->
  <circle cx="70"  cy="129" r="4" fill="#FB923C"/>
  <circle cx="340" cy="185" r="4" fill="#FB923C"/>
  <circle cx="610" cy="217" r="4" fill="#FB923C"/>

  <!-- Value Labels -->
  <text x="78"  y="68"  font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#1E3A5F">0.72</text>
  <text x="348" y="135" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#1E3A5F">0.47</text>
  <text x="540" y="170" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="10" fill="#1E3A5F">0.34</text>

  <!-- Legend -->
  <rect x="420" y="38" width="180" height="52" fill="white" stroke="#E5E7EB" stroke-width="0.8" rx="6" opacity="0.95"/>
  <line x1="430" y1="56" x2="454" y2="56" stroke="#2563EB" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <circle cx="442" cy="56" r="4" fill="#2563EB"/>
  <text x="460" y="60" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#374151">SI(Calcite)</text>
  <line x1="430" y1="76" x2="454" y2="76" stroke="#FB923C" stroke-width="1.8" stroke-dasharray="7 3"/>
  <circle cx="442" cy="76" r="3" fill="#FB923C"/>
  <text x="460" y="80" font-family="'Segoe UI',sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#374151">SI(Aragonite)</text>
</svg>
<p style="text-align:center; font-size:0.82em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); margin-top:0.5em;">Figure 2. Decrease in SI(Calcite) as Hot Spring Water Cools — Even as the SI decreases, it remains in the supersaturated zone, meaning scaling risk persists.</p>
</div>
```

::: callout-important
## Why does the SI drop as temperature drops? (The impact of excess Carbon)
Calcite is known for its **retrograde solubility**, meaning it usually becomes *less* soluble as temperature increases, and *more* soluble as it cools. 
However, in our Python code, we only provided an initial `Alkalinity` of 12 mmol/L as the carbon source. **We did not add excess `C(4)` (like CO₂ gas from the atmosphere).**

Under these closed conditions, as the water cools, CO₂ becomes more soluble (Henry's Law), causing the balance to shift towards aqueous $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3^*$. This causes $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ to shift toward the $\text{HCO}_3^-$ side (association) and decrease in concentration. The resulting drop in IAP leads to the **SI decreasing alongside temperature** (which looks like normal solubility).

Retrograde behavior becomes strongly apparent in **open systems with fixed $p\text{CO}_2$** (e.g., exposed to the atmosphere) or in **closed systems with very high initial total carbon**. Accurate scale diagnosis requires precise knowledge of the field's $p\text{CO}_2$ conditions!
:::

---

## Application 2: Diagnosing Water Types by "Reading" SI Patterns

By looking at the pattern of Saturation Indices, you can deduce the geological history of the water.

```{=html}
<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.2em; margin:1.5em 0;">

  <div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:var(--color-text-primary); margin-bottom:0.8em; border-bottom:2px solid #D97706; padding-bottom:0.4em;">🏔️ Limestone Groundwater</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.87em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); line-height:1.8;">
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Calcite)   = +0.15 (Near Eq)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Quartz)    = +0.18 (Near Eq)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#2563EB; font-weight:600;">SI(Kaolinite) = +2.64 (Supersat)</span><br><br>
      Ca–HCO₃ type water. It is close to equilibrium with calcite. The longer the residence time, the closer SI(Calcite) gets to 0. Kaolinite supersaturation indicates ongoing feldspar weathering into clay.
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:var(--color-text-primary); margin-bottom:0.8em; border-bottom:2px solid #D97706; padding-bottom:0.4em;">🪨 Granite Groundwater</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.87em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); line-height:1.8;">
      <span style="color:#DC2626;">SI(Calcite)   = −2.23 (Undersat)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Quartz)    = +0.69 (Supersat)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#2563EB; font-weight:600;">SI(Kaolinite) = +6.96 (Extreme Supersat)</span><br><br>
      Na–HCO₃ type. Weathering of feldspar supplies Na, Si, and Al. The massive SI for Kaolinite is a hallmark of active feldspar weathering and clay formation.
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:var(--color-text-primary); margin-bottom:0.8em; border-bottom:2px solid #EA580C; padding-bottom:0.4em;">♨️ Carbonate Hot Spring</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.87em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); line-height:1.8;">
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Calcite)   = +0.65 (Scale Risk)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Quartz)    = +0.64 (Supersat)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#2563EB; font-weight:600;">SI(Kaolinite) = +2.76 (Strong Supersat)</span><br><br>
      High alkalinity (12 mmol/L) drives carbonate supersaturation. Extreme caution is needed for calcite scaling in pipes and heat exchangers.
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="background:var(--color-background-secondary); border:1px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius:10px; padding:1.2em;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:var(--color-text-primary); margin-bottom:0.8em; border-bottom:2px solid #2563EB; padding-bottom:0.4em;">🌊 Seawater</div>
    <div style="font-size:0.87em; color:var(--color-text-secondary); line-height:1.8;">
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Calcite)   = +0.72 (Supersat)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#16A34A; font-weight:600;">SI(Quartz)    = +0.04 (Near Eq)</span><br>
      <span style="color:#DC2626;">SI(Kaolinite) = −2.77 (Undersat)</span><br><br>
      Carbonates are supersaturated but rarely precipitate spontaneously without biological intervention. Due to high ionic strength, Al activity is suppressed, keeping Kaolinite undersaturated.
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

---

## Uncertainties: Handling Measurement Errors

::: callout-note
## The Acceptable Range for SI $\approx$ 0
A field measurement error of ±0.1 in pH translates directly to roughly ±0.1 uncertainty in SI. A temperature error of ±2°C causes an SI variance of ±0.1 to 0.2 in carbonate systems. Therefore, practically speaking, **any SI between -0.2 and +0.2 should be interpreted as "essentially at equilibrium."**
:::

::: {style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.2em 0;"}
| SI Range | Geochemical Meaning | Practical Interpretation |
|------------------|------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **SI < -1** | Strongly Undersaturated | Fast dissolution for carbonates / Kinetic limits for silicates |
| **-1 ≤ SI < -0.2** | Undersaturated | No scaling; slow long-term mineral depletion |
| **-0.2 ≤ SI ≤ +0.2** | Near Equilibrium | The mineral is likely controlling the system chemistry |
| **+0.2 < SI ≤ +1** | Mildly Supersaturated | Potential precipitation (scaling starts for carbonates) |
| **SI > +1** | Strongly Supersaturated | High scaling risk / Extreme thermodynamic drive (though kinetics may still limit actual precipitation) |
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
